Capacity (`accommodates`), room type, and the number of bathrooms account for most of the explainable variation in price across all models that include them. The GAM shows that an additional bathroom adds roughly 25% to the price. A private room sells for about 30% less than an entire home, all else equal. Reviews and host attributes matter, but their effect is small compared to the physical features a host can describe in a listing.

A contrast worth highlighting is the Naive ANN versus the Expanded ANN. The naive version, trained only on host variables, predicts a *lower* price for Superhosts (**`r results.ann_basic$Predicted_Price_USD[2]` USD** vs. **`r results.ann_basic$Predicted_Price_USD[1]` USD**). Once room and property type are added, the gap collapses to less than a dollar. This isn't really a disagreement between models but a useful warning against drawing conclusions from raw price comparisons.

The answer the *first research question* is relatively straightforward. We find that virtually all of the variables we examined have an impact on pricing. Interestingly, Superhost status has a negative impact. Furthermore, we find that venue-oriented variables, in particular location and physical features, have the strongest influence.

To answer the *second research question*, we draw on both the GAM and the SVM. The GAM itself explains `r gam_dev_expl`% of the deviance on held-out data and generates a fair-market price for every listing, strong evidence that the price drivers we identified are predictive, not just descriptive. The traffic-light verdicts derived from the GAM feed directly into the SVM, which reaches `r round(svm_accuracy * 100, 2)`% accuracy on the test set. We can predict price fairness, but we also recognize that this accuracy is insufficient to criticize hosts for their pricing based on the variables available to us.


### Overall Limitations

Five limitations apply to this entire study, not just to individual
models:

**Snapshot, no time component**. All models are trained on a single
point in time. Seasonal pricing, weekday effects, and event-driven
demand spikes (Montreux Jazz Festival, ski season, weekends) are nowhere
in the data. The recommendations represent an *average* market price,
not a dynamic one. A real production pricing tool would need a time
dimension we don't have.

**Listed price as a proxy for willingness to pay**. We treat what hosts
charge as a proxy for what guests would pay. This holds on average
across a large sample, but it breaks down for listings that sit unbooked
at their current price, those listings still shape the model and
probably tilt it slightly toward optimistic price levels. A true
willingness-to-pay study would need booking data, completed
transactions, and ideally A/B tests of competing prices.

**Missing predictors.** Photo quality, amenity counts, listing
description sentiment, view from the property, noise levels, distance to
public transport, none of this is in the dataset. Some of the
unexplained variance (around a third for the GAM) lives in these omitted
variables rather than in any model's limitation.

**Reverse causality.** Several relationships in our data probably flow
in both directions. More bookings produce more reviews, which make
Superhost status easier to maintain, which probably increases
visibility, which leads to more bookings. None of our models can
untangle which effect causes which. For descriptive purposes that's
fine, but the recommendations should be read as "what works on average,"
not "what works because of X."

**Vaud-specific.** The dataset covers one Swiss canton at one point in
time. The price levels, location dynamics (lakeshore premium, alpine
resorts), and host behaviours are specific to this market. The
methodology transfers; the exact coefficients do not. Applying the same
models to Geneva or Bern would require refitting on local data.

### Next Steps
When we put the results of the study and the identified limitations into context, we realize that there is plenty of room for future research. We were able to see that a generalized additive model is a very useful approach. However, we have a clear shortcoming, particularly with regard to the limitation of location. Especially if there is a meaningful operationalization of location, for example, based on distances to a city center—pricing can be addressed in even greater detail. This would benefit not only the GAM but all models. However, our study demonstrated what is fundamentally possible. Regarding the second research question and the support vector machine, we also recognize that price classification is both possible and useful. Therefore, we see massive potential for Airbnb in this area in particular, which could be explored in a future study.
Further improvements are also possible if the dataset were expanded to include additional Swiss cantons or other parts of the world. 


## Businesscase conclusion
### Recommendations for Hosts

For hosts setting or revising their nightly price:

-   **Anchor on structure, not status**. What a guest actually pays for
    is the listing itself: room type, capacity, bathrooms, location. The
    Superhost badge helps with visibility and booking volume, not with
    the per-night rate.
-   **Use the traffic-light tool as a sanity check, not a target**. The
    GAM-based recommendation gives a fair-market range based on what
    comparable listings achieve. A listing flagged "Underpriced" is
    leaving money on the table; one flagged "Overpriced" risks sitting
    empty.
-   **If you're building toward Superhost status, compete on price**.
    The 2.6× occupancy boost comes from satisfied guests and a full
    calendar. Premium pricing makes both harder. Start with competitive
    pricing, accumulate reviews, then raise prices selectively once the
    badge is secure.
-   **Review scores matter less than you think for price**. Across the
    pricing models the reward for higher ratings is modest. Reviews
    matter, but their role is to drive *bookings*.

### Recommendations for Airbnb

For the platform itself, the same data points in three directions:

-   **A pricing assistant should preferably use the GAM, not a linear formula**.
    Linear or postcode-based tools will systematically underprice
    lakeshore listings and overprice rural ones. The 2D spatial spline
    is the most defensible way to capture local dynamics with the data
    Airbnb already collects.
-   **Coach the "Underpriced" segment**. Roughly 41% of listings in the
    dataset sit below the GAM's fair-price band. These hosts are the
    easiest client group to help. A recommendation alone could shift their
    pricing toward market value, lifting both host revenue and platform
    commission.
-   **Add operationalised location features**. Raw coordinates work well
    for a smoother like the GAM but limit simpler tools. Distance to the
    nearest lake, city centre, ski lift, and public transport stop would
    let classifiers like the SVM catch up to the GAM's accuracy — and
    these features are trivial to compute centrally and serve to every
    model on the platform.




# Lessons learned

## Pascal

Working on this project was a huge reality check in the best way
possible. In our classes, we are used to getting "perfect" datasets that
are practically made for the specific algorithms we are learning. But
working with our actual `d.airbnb_listings_feat` dataset showed me how
much effort actually goes into data cleaning and prepping before you can
even start modeling. I personally focused on building the GLM-Poisson
and ANN models, and trying to get them to perform well on real, messy
data required a lot more critical thinking and trial-and-error than our
standard assignments in class.

Since we split up the workload, I didn't code every single model myself.
Instead, my role for the LM, GLM-Binomial, GAM, and SVM models was to
check and review the work of my team members. This turned out to be a
super valuable learning experience on its own. Reading through their
code allowed me to see exactly how other people translate what we
learned in class into practice. Seeing their different approaches to
problem-solving and troubleshooting bugs really helped expand my own
coding mindset.

Finally, figuring out how to collaborate effectively on code was a
massive win for us. We used GitHub with RStudio for the first time, and
it honestly made a world of difference. We finally found a setup that
actually works for coding together as a group, which saved us a lot of
headaches and is definitely a workflow I’ll be taking into future
projects.

## Timon

I was already familiar with RStudio from my bachelor’s program, and I’d
had very positive experiences with it so far and I feel very comfortable
using it. Up until now, however, I’ve mainly worked with simple linear
models and generalized linear models. Everything else was new to me. In
this project, I focused primarily on support vector machines, and I’m
very impressed by the concept because it’s both very simple and yet
involves a tremendous amount of complexity. Working with this model in
particular was a key learning experience for me.

Another key learning was the SCRUM-like structuring of the work process.
Within the document, we work with a list of tasks to be completed by the
next meeting and a backlog of tasks that we can’t tackle right now but
know are coming up. This worked very well for us. It provided a sense of
accountability to ensure all tasks were completed effectively.

Finally, working with GitHub on this scale was also fairly new to me. We
used the integration with RStudio for the first time, and I’m personally
thrilled by how simple it is. We had almost no issues with incorrect
versions, even though we weren’t working with separate branches. I
think, however, that splitting up the models really helped us in that
regard.

## Davide

Coming into this project, my comfort zone was firmly in theory. I knew
what a GLM was on paper, but actually building one that holds up against
messy data was a different experience. Working on the GLM Binomial and GAM
forced me to confront the gap between understanding a model and making
it useful. Things that look elegant in a lecture slide turn out to
require dozens of small judgement calls in practice. Questions like which variables to include, how to handle NAs, when to log-transform, where to draw a
reasonable threshold and much more.

The GAM was probably my biggest single learning. I did not worked a lot with
smooth terms before, and seeing the 2D spatial spline pick up Vaud's
geography was genuinely a moment where the theory clicked into practice.

I went into this project assuming the modelling would be the hard part. By the end, communicating the results clearly turned out to be just as much work.

Finally, R Bootcamp in February laid the foundation, but this project
was where I actually got comfortable in R. Reading the others' code,
debugging together, learning shortcuts and much more. That side-by-side learning was
something I can't get from several tutorials.


# Use of GenAI

Throughout this machine learning project, we leveraged various
Generative AI tools specifically ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude to support
our analytical workflow. A primary use case was troubleshooting complex
issues that extended beyond our existing knowledge base. When
encountering technical bottlenecks, we used these models to break down
and explain the underlying concepts, enabling us to systematically find
the right solutions. Furthermore, we utilized GenAI as a critical review
mechanism for our modeling approach. We consulted the AI to verify
whether we had made the appropriate theoretical assumptions, such as
selecting the correct data distributions for our models. It also served
as a valuable sounding board to double-check our final analytical
interpretations, ensuring our conclusions were statistically sound.
Finally, these tools provided essential linguistic support. We actively
used GenAI to translate our documentation and findings into professional
English, ensuring a high-quality final report.
